name: Hinge #1: Feeling-thermometer measurement validity
description: A's two supporting arguments stand; B's methodological critique was undermined by Iyengar's response, but an open qualifier survives.
purpose: Whether feeling-thermometer scales are a valid instrument for measuring affective polarization.

argument.text: Feeling-thermometer scales validly measure affective polarization in U.S. survey panels.

conclusion.text: Feeling-thermometer scales validly measure affective polarization in U.S. survey panels.

claim.text: Boxell, Gentzkow, and Shapiro (2024) validated the thermometer scale across multiple ANES waves.
The scale exhibits high test-retest reliability within respondents.

scheme.name: Argument from Expert Opinion
scheme.description: An expert E in domain D asserts that A; therefore A.
cq: Is the source a genuine expert in the relevant field?
Is the assertion within the expert's domain of expertise?
Are there other experts who disagree?

argument.text: Cross-wave ANES panel data show stable thermometer differentials between in-party and out-party targets.

conclusion.text: ANES panel data show stable in-party / out-party thermometer differentials over multiple waves.

claim.text: Differentials are robust to respondent-fixed-effects specifications.

scheme.name: Argument from Evidence
scheme.description: Empirical data E supports claim C.

argument.text: Druckman and colleagues argue that feeling thermometers conflate ideological disagreement with personal animus, so high thermometer gaps may not isolate affective polarization.

conclusion.text: Feeling-thermometer gaps conflate ideological disagreement with personal animus.

claim.text: Survey-experimental manipulations of policy framing shift thermometer ratings independent of personal-affect priming.

scheme.name: Argument from Expert Opinion
scheme.description: An expert E in domain D asserts that A; therefore A.
cq: Is the source a genuine expert in the relevant field?
Are there other experts who disagree?

argument.text: Iyengar et al. distinguish ideological from affective components using paired thermometer + trait batteries, showing the affective component remains after partialling out ideology.

conclusion.text: Paired thermometer + trait batteries isolate an affective component that survives ideology controls.

claim.text: Trait-rating supplements reduce shared variance with ideology to under 30%.

scheme.name: Argument from Evidence
scheme.description: Empirical data E supports claim C.

argument.text: A mixed measurement strategy (thermometers paired with trait batteries) improves construct validity, though a residual band of contested cases remains where ideology and affect cannot be cleanly separated.

conclusion.text: A mixed-measurement strategy improves validity; a residual contested band persists.

claim.text: No single instrument disambiguates ideology from affect in highly co-partisan respondents.

scheme.name: Practical Reasoning
scheme.description: If goal G and action A achieves G, then do A.
cq: Are there alternative means to G?

edges.description: ANES panel evidence directly supports the validity claim.

argument.text: Cross-wave ANES panel data show stable thermometer differentials between in-party and out-party targets.

conclusion.text: ANES panel data show stable in-party / out-party thermometer differentials over multiple waves.

argument.text: Feeling-thermometer scales validly measure affective polarization in U.S. survey panels.

conclusion.text: Feeling-thermometer scales validly measure affective polarization in U.S. survey panels.

edges.description: Druckman's measurement critique directly attacks the validity claim.

argument.text: Druckman and colleagues argue that feeling thermometers conflate ideological disagreement with personal animus.

conclusion.text: Feeling-thermometer gaps conflate ideological disagreement with personal animus.

argument.text: Feeling-thermometer scales validly measure affective polarization in U.S. survey panels.

conclusion.text: Feeling-thermometer scales validly measure affective polarization in U.S. survey panels.

edges.description: Iyengar's paired-instrument design undercuts the conflation premise.

argument.text: Iyengar et al. distinguish ideological from affective components using paired thermometer + trait batteries.

conclusion.text: Paired thermometer + trait batteries isolate an affective component that survives ideology controls.

argument.text: Druckman and colleagues argue that feeling thermometers conflate ideological disagreement with personal animus.

conclusion.text: Feeling-thermometer gaps conflate ideological disagreement with personal animus.

edges.description: Mixed-measurement qualifier narrows the scope of the validity claim.

argument.text: A mixed measurement strategy improves construct validity, though a residual band of contested cases remains.

conclusion.text: A mixed-measurement strategy improves validity; a residual contested band persists.

argument.text: Feeling-thermometer scales validly measure affective polarization in U.S. survey panels.

conclusion.text: Feeling-thermometer scales validly measure affective polarization in U.S. survey panels.
